notes.anchor: on the seventh day before the Kalends of December
notes.note: Roman dates count inclusively backward from the next named day; the Kalends is the 1st of December, so the seventh day before it (counting both ends) is 24 November. Cicero is landing back in Italy from his province.

notes.anchor: such a pretty little spondaic breeze blew us from Epirus --- the gentlest Onchesmite
notes.note: Onchesmus was a harbour on the coast of Epirus; Cicero coins a mock-epic joke, calling the favourable wind from there an 'Onchesmite' and noting that the phrase scans as a spondaic (ending-in-spondees) line of verse.

notes.anchor: one of the quartans has gone and the other is coming on more lightly
notes.note: A quartan is a recurrent fever (typically malarial) that returns every fourth day by inclusive reckoning; Atticus was suffering a double quartan, with attacks on successive days, which Cicero hopes is now abating.
Trebula was a town in Campania; the 'Trebulan' estate where Pontius gave the news belonged to Pontius himself, one of the country houses along Cicero's route up the Italian coast.

notes.anchor: I left Tiro sick at Patrae
notes.note: Tiro was Cicero's trusted secretary (later his freedman and the editor of his letters); Patrae (modern Patras) was the Achaean port where the homeward party put in, and Cicero left the ailing Tiro there in Curius's care.

notes.anchor: sealed with the signets of the three Ciceros and of the praetorian cohort
notes.note: A Roman will required seven witnesses' seals to be valid; here Cicero, his son, and his nephew (the 'three Ciceros') plus members of the governor's praetorian cohort (his official staff/bodyguard) supplied the witnessing seals.

notes.anchor: his heir of an as and me of a quarter-as
notes.note: Roman wills divided the estate into twelfths of an as (the unit coin): a libella was one-tenth and a teruncius a quarter, so the figures are token honorary bequests marking esteem rather than real shares.

notes.anchor: an account of Pompey's conversation at Naples
notes.note: Pompey was convalescing near Naples in 50 BC; Atticus had sounded him out and reported his friendly talk about Cicero's standing and his hoped-for triumph, news Cicero found especially welcome.

notes.anchor: those most impudent letters of Bibulus --- which were followed by the most lavish supplication possible
notes.note: M. Calpurnius Bibulus, governor of Syria, won an exceptionally long public thanksgiving (supplicatio) for victories Cicero thinks exaggerated, since Bibulus never left his fortifications; Cicero is galled that his own real campaigning earned less.

notes.anchor: so long as the enemy was on this side of the Euphrates
notes.note: The Euphrates was the frontier between Roman Syria and Parthia; while the Parthians remained west of it (on the Roman side) Bibulus stayed shut in his stronghold, in Cicero's pointed contrast with his own activity.

notes.anchor: this same Cato voted Bibulus twenty days
notes.note: The number of days of a supplicatio measured the honour; Cato proposed twenty days' thanksgiving for his son-in-law Bibulus while voting against any for Cicero, the partiality that so stings Cicero here.

notes.anchor: that old formula of Drusus the praetor
notes.note: M. Livius Drusus, a noted jurist-praetor, was famous for a ruling about manumission oaths; Cicero invokes it to argue that his runaway slave Chrysippus was never legally freed, since no one was present to claim his freedom on his behalf.

notes.anchor: the one in which you discuss the dangers facing the commonwealth
notes.note: Atticus had written analysing the political peril of the Caesar-Pompey crisis; Cicero, overwhelmed, leaves it unanswered, deflecting with the grim joke that the Parthians' near-fatal attack on Bibulus gives him less to fear at home.
